title: Security Analysis
author: Benjamin Graham & David L. Dodd
subtitle: Principles and Technique of Security Valuation
description: The foundational text of value investing, first published in 1934 and revised across six editions through 2008. Graham and Dodd established the methodology for evaluating securities through fundamental analysis — bond and preferred stock evaluation, common stock intrinsic value estimation, and the discipline of buying at a substantial margin below intrinsic value. Warren Buffett called it 'a roadmap for investing that I have now been following for 47 years.'
keyIdeas: Intrinsic value is the single most important concept in security analysis
Margin of safety: buy substantially below intrinsic value to account for error
Mr. Market: view market fluctuations as a manic-depressive partner, not a guide
Bond analysis: focus on earning power, not just asset coverage
Preferred stock is a bond with equity characteristics — analyze it as such
Common stock valuation: asset value, earning power, and dividend power tests
Graham-Dodd defensive investor criteria: diversification, quality, and price
The enterprising investor pursues higher returns through greater effort
Railroads, utilities, and industrials have distinct valuation methods
Defensive vs. enterprising: investors must choose their approach
keyTakeaways: Intrinsic value is the present value of all future income — not a single metric
Always buy with a margin of safety — at least one-third below intrinsic value
Treat the market as a manic-depressive partner: profit from its irrationality
Bond quality depends on earnings coverage, not just asset collateral
Callable bonds are worth less than non-callable equivalents — deduct the call option
Preferred stocks deserve bond-level scrutiny because they have bond-like obligations
Common stocks should be evaluated based on earning power, not just liquidating value
The defensive investor needs a simple, quantitative, rules-based approach
The enterprising investor must be willing to do the work that securities analysis requires
Financial statements are the primary tool — know how to read and interpret them
whoShouldRead: Value investors who want to understand the intellectual foundation of the approach
Finance students who need the original source rather than derivative summaries
CFA candidates studying equity and fixed income valuation
Professional analysts who want to revisit first principles
Anyone who believes the market can be beaten through discipline and analysis

relatedBooks.title: The Little Book That Beats the Market
